CQF Module 4 - Report¶

Anjeshnu Trivedi
June 2025 Cohort

Part A — Maths¶

A1¶

The Radial Basis Function (RBF) kernel, also known as the Gaussian kernel, is a similarity measure that enables Support Vector Machines (SVMs) to classify data that is not linearly separable in the original feature space.

It is defined as: $$ 𝐾 (𝑥_𝑖, x_j) = \exp (- \gamma ||x_i - x_j||^2) $$ where: $ x_i, x_j $ are feature vectors, $ ||x_i - x_j||^2 $ is the squared Euclidean distance, and $\gamma > 0$ controls the “spread” of the kernel.

The RBF kernel effectively maps data into an infinite-dimensional feature space without ever computing those coordinates explicitly — this is known as the kernel trick.

Intuition:

Each training observation defines a local region of influence; nearby points (in feature space) have high kernel values, while distant points contribute little. The parameter $\gamma$ determines how local or global that influence is:

  • High $\gamma$: very localized (each support vector covers a small region) → can lead to overfitting.
  • Low $\gamma$: smoother, more global decision boundary → can lead to underfitting.

Thus, the RBF kernel allows SVMs to capture nonlinear relationships by letting the model form flexible, curved decision boundaries that separate classes based on distance similarity rather than linear projection.

Solution: (a)

Plugging the values of $z_1 and z_2$ in the given RBF Gaussian Kernel $ k (𝑥_𝑖, x_j) = \exp (- \frac{||x_i - x_j||^2}{2 \sigma}) $, the the expression becomes $\exp(- \frac{||z_1 - x||^2}{2 \sigma})$ for $z_1$ and $\exp(- \frac{||z_2 - x||^2}{2 \sigma})$ for $z_2$.

Since $z_1$ is close to x hence $z_1 - x -> 0$ as a result $\exp(- \frac{||z_1 - x||^2}{2 \sigma}) -> 1$ as $\exp(0)$ = 1.

A2¶

A voting classifier is an ensemble learning method that combines the predictions of multiple base models to produce a final decision. The idea is simple — aggregate diverse learners so that individual errors cancel out and the ensemble achieves higher overall accuracy and robustness.

There are two main types:

Hard Voting: Each base model outputs a class label (e.g. 0 or 1). The ensemble predicts the class that receives the majority of votes.

$$ \hat{y} = \text{mode}\{ h_1(x),\, h_2(x),\, \ldots,\, h_m(x) \} $$

Soft Voting: Each model outputs class probabilities $p_k (x)$ These probabilities are averaged (optionally weighted) and the class with the highest mean probability is chosen. $$ \hat{y} = \arg \max_j \sum_i w_i \, p_{ij}(x) $$

Advantages

  • Reduces variance and model-specific bias.
  • Improves stability and generalization, especially when models are heterogeneous (e.g. SVM + Tree + Logistic Regression).
  • Soft voting allows weighting by validation performance or confidence.

Limitations

  • Less interpretable.
  • Gains diminish if models are highly correlated.
  • Computationally heavier and sensitive to poor probability calibration in soft voting.

Data Preparation and Feature Initialization¶

Overview¶

To construct an explanatory and predictive dataset for the QQQ ETF (Nasdaq-100 tracker), raw daily OHLCV (Open, High, Low, Close, Volume) data were combined with cross-asset indicators representing major macro and risk factors.
The objective was to create a structured feature space capturing price dynamics, volatility, momentum, and cross-market sentiment.

The following assets were used as inputs:

  • QQQ — Nasdaq-100 ETF (target asset)
  • S&P 500 Index — Broad market benchmark
  • VIX — Equity market volatility index
  • TLT — Long-term U.S. Treasury bond ETF (interest-rate proxy)
  • Crude Oil — Energy / inflation proxy
  • Gold — Safe-haven / inflation hedge

Each dataset was aligned to a common business-day calendar (2005-2025) and forward-filled to handle missing values.

Base Feature Construction¶

For each asset, several time-series transformations were computed to capture different market dimensions:

Category Feature Description
Returns & Momentum ret_1, ret_5, logret_1 1-day, 5-day, and log returns capturing short-term momentum and volatility bursts.
Moving Averages ema_5, ema_20, ema_ratio_5_20, sma_5, sma_20, sma_ratio_5_20 Exponential and simple moving averages (5 vs 20 days) used to identify short-term vs. medium-term trend direction.
Volatility Metrics vol_10, vol_20 Rolling standard deviation of daily returns over 10 and 20 days.
Average True Range (ATR) atr_5, atr_20 Measures short-term and medium-term trading-range volatility.
Volume Activity vol_z 20-day z-score of trading volume, used as a proxy for participation intensity.
Relative Strength Index (RSI) rsi_5, rsi_20 Momentum oscillator indicating overbought/oversold conditions on short and medium horizons.
Bollinger Bands LB_5, UB_5, LB_20, UB_20 Dynamic volatility envelopes around the moving average to track extreme price deviations.

Cross-Asset and Composite Features¶

To enrich the feature space with macro-financial context, several composite features were derived linking different assets:

Feature Formula Economic Interpretation
Relative Strength (Nasdaq–S&P) qqq_ret_5 − spx_ret_5 Measures technology sector outperformance vs. the broad market.
Risk Aversion Proxy vix_logret_1 − tlt_logret_1 Captures investor risk-off sentiment: higher VIX and rising bond prices imply market stress.
Inflation Beta crude_ret_5 − gold_ret_5 Differentiates between growth/inflation (oil) and defensive inflation hedge (gold).

These engineered indicators integrate equity momentum, rates, volatility, and commodities into a unified framework for machine-learning analysis.

Final Dataset Structure¶

After feature generation:

  • Each column was prefixed with its asset key (e.g., qqq_close, vix_logret_1, tlt_vol_20) for clarity.
  • The dataset was forward-filled, aligned on trading dates, and standardized for analysis.

  • The target variable (y) was defined as a binary indicator of positive next-day QQQ return, using a threshold of $0.5\%$: $$

    yt = \begin{cases} 1, & \text{if } r{t+1} > 0.5\% \ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} $$ This target setup transforms the task into a binary classification problem — predicting short-term uptrends.

Summary Data Initialization¶

The initialization process resulted in a rich, multi-asset feature matrix capturing:

  • Price momentum and trend signals,
  • Volatility and volume dynamics,
  • Cross-asset macro relationships.

This feature universe served as the input for the feature-selection funnel and subsequent SVM modeling steps in Parts B and C.

Part B — Feature Selection using the Funnelling Approach¶

B.1 Objective & Data Scope¶

We aim to identify a compact, informative subset of predictors for daily QQQ up/down moves.
All modeling and selection use data from 2015-01-01 onward. The binary target is defined with a +0.50% next-day return threshold: $ y_{t}=\mathbb{1}\{ r_{t+1} > 0.005 \}. $

The feature universe includes:

  • Target asset (QQQ) technicals: returns, moving averages, volatility, RSI, ATR, Bollinger bands, volume z-score.
  • Cross-asset context: S&P 500 (broad market), VIX (equity vol), TLT (rates proxy), Crude (inflation/growth), Gold (defensive hedge).
  • Composite signals: relative strength (QQQ vs SPX), risk-aversion proxy (VIX–TLT), inflation beta (Crude–Gold).

B.2 Filter Step — Mutual Information¶

Method. We computed Mutual Information (mutual_info_classif) between each feature and y to capture non-linear relevance at low cost. The Mutual Information (MI) criterion measures the statistical dependency between each individual feature and the binary target variable.
Unlike correlation, MI captures both linear and nonlinear relationships, estimating how much knowing a feature reduces uncertainty about the target (up/down movement of QQQ).

Mathematically, the mutual information between a feature ( X ) and target ( Y ) is: $$ I(X; Y) = \sum_{x \in X} \sum_{y \in Y} p(x,y) \log \frac{p(x,y)}{p(x)p(y)}. $$ A higher MI score indicates stronger relevance — the feature provides more information about the target direction.
We computed MI scores using mutual_info_classif from scikit-learn and selected the Top 40 features as inputs to the wrapper (RFE) step.

Selection. Kept the Top-N features (N = 40) as the initial shortlist for the wrapper stage.

Key observations:

  • Dominance of QQQ-specific features. Most high-MI variables relate directly to the QQQ ETF’s own price dynamics—particularly its short- to medium-term trend (qqq_ema_20, qqq_sma_20) and volatility (qqq_vol_10, qqq_lb_5, qqq_atr_20).
    This suggests that local trend and dispersion measures remain the strongest immediate drivers of next-day direction.
  • Volatility and risk sentiment effects. VIX-related measures (vix_level, vix_ret_5, vix_ret_1, vix_logret_1) appear consistently within the top quartile, confirming that implied volatility conditions materially influence short-term QQQ behavior.
  • Cross-asset signals show moderate influence. Features from bonds (tlt_level, tlt_logret_1) and commodities (gold_ret_5, crudeoil_ret_5) exhibit lower MI values but still provide incremental predictive context.
  • Composite indicators weakly ranked. Features such as rel_strength_nasdaq_spx, risk_aversion_proxy, and inflation_beta scored among the lowest, implying that broad macro-relative relationships contribute less to one-day-ahead prediction than direct price or volatility metrics.
  • Overall insight. The MI ranking highlights that short-term predictability is largely contained within intra-asset technical signals (momentum, volatility, and range-based features), while cross-asset and composite measures appear more relevant for longer horizons or regime classification tasks. Overall, the MI filter step helped narrow the feature space to those variables most informative about QQQ’s directional movement, balancing momentum, volatility, and cross-asset perspectives before moving into wrapper-based refinement.

Table B2 (MI ranking).
Top 10 filter features:

Rank Feature MI Score
1 qqq_ema_20 0.0273
2 vix_level 0.0271
3 qqq_vol_10 0.0251
4 qqq_lb_5 0.0228
5 vix_ret_5 0.0210
6–10 qqq_sma_20, qqq_open, qqq_sma_5, snp500_ret_5, tlt_level 0.018–0.014

Figure B2.1 — Top 20 Features Ranked by Mutual Information

Selected features (MI-40): 'qqq_ema_20', 'vix_level', 'qqq_vol_10', 'qqq_lb_5', 'vix_ret_5', 'qqq_sma_20', 'qqq_open', 'qqq_sma_5', 'snp500_ret_5', 'tlt_level', 'qqq_low', 'gold_ret_5', 'qqq_atr_20', 'qqq_volume', 'qqq_vol_20', 'vix_ret_1', 'gold_level', 'crudeoil_ret_5', 'qqq_ub_5', 'vix_logret_1', 'qqq_ema_5', 'qqq_atr_5', 'qqq_high', 'snp500_level', 'qqq_rsi_20', 'qqq_lb_20', 'qqq_ret_1', 'rel_strength_nasdaq_spx', 'qqq_logret_1', 'qqq_vol_z', 'tlt_logret_1', 'snp500_ret_1', 'tlt_ret_5', 'qqq_ub_20', 'risk_aversion_proxy', 'qqq_ret_5', 'crudeoil_level', 'gold_logret_1', 'gold_ret_1', 'crudeoil_ret_1'

B.3 Wrapper Step — Recursive Feature Elimination (RFE)¶

Estimator. Logistic Regression (class_weight='balanced', liblinear).
Rationale. RFE iteratively removes the least important features (by absolute coefficient under scaling), reducing redundancy within correlated groups. The Recursive Feature Elimination (RFE) approach applies a supervised, model-based wrapper method to iteratively rank features by predictive contribution.
Using a Logistic Regression classifier (class_weight='balanced') as the base estimator, the algorithm fits the model repeatedly, removing the weakest features (by absolute coefficient magnitude) at each iteration. This process continues until the desired number of features remains.
The model was trained on the filtered feature subset from the MI step, with median imputation and standardization applied beforehand.
The final selection retained 25 features after recursive elimination.
Preprocessing. Median imputation and standardization performed before RFE.

Setting. Targeted K = 25 features.
Selected features (RFE-25):
'qqq_ema_20', 'vix_level', 'qqq_lb_5', 'qqq_sma_20', 'qqq_open', 'qqq_sma_5', 'qqq_low', 'qqq_atr_20', 'qqq_volume', 'qqq_vol_20', 'vix_ret_1', 'qqq_ub_5', 'vix_logret_1', 'qqq_ema_5', 'qqq_high', 'snp500_level', 'qqq_rsi_20', 'qqq_lb_20', 'qqq_ret_1', 'qqq_logret_1', 'qqq_vol_z', 'snp500_ret_1', 'tlt_ret_5', 'qqq_ub_20', 'risk_aversion_proxy'

Key observations:

  • Dominance of price- and trend-based features.
    Most retained variables belong to QQQ’s own technical domain — moving averages (qqq_ema_20, qqq_sma_20, qqq_ema_5, qqq_sma_5), high/low/close levels, and derived volatility or band measures (qqq_lb_5, qqq_ub_5, qqq_lb_20, qqq_ub_20).
    This indicates that RFE emphasized internal market structure and local momentum over external signals.
  • Volatility and range indicators remained important.
    Features such as qqq_atr_20, qqq_vol_20, and qqq_vol_z were consistently retained, reflecting the predictive relevance of realized volatility and trading-range compression/expansion in short-term direction.
  • Cross-asset sentiment captured via VIX and bonds.
    vix_level, vix_ret_1, and vix_logret_1 survived multiple elimination rounds, underscoring volatility sentiment as a key driver.
    tlt_ret_5 adds a fixed-income risk-off dimension, complementing the VIX components.
  • Broader market alignment.
    Inclusion of snp500_level and snp500_ret_1 suggests that RFE preserved features reflecting overall equity market co-movement, supporting the idea that QQQ’s direction is influenced by broad-index momentum.
  • Limited composite influence.
    Only one composite metric (risk_aversion_proxy) was retained, implying that while cross-asset spreads provide contextual information, their marginal predictive power is weaker than direct volatility or momentum inputs.
  • Reduced redundancy and improved stability.
    Strongly collinear or overlapping indicators were pruned (e.g., several QQQ return variants), yielding a more parsimonious and interpretable subset for the embedded (L1) stage.

B.4 Embedded Step — L1 Regularization¶

Estimator. LogisticRegressionCV with L1 penalty; TimeSeriesSplit cross-validation; wide grid of C to avoid over-sparsification; scoring = ROC-AUC (or F1).
Rationale. L1 encourages sparsity by shrinking weak/unstable coefficients to exact zero, yielding a smaller, more interpretable set. The embedded feature selection step applies an L1-penalized Logistic Regression (LogisticRegressionCV) with TimeSeriesSplit cross-validation and a wide grid of C values to balance sparsity against predictive power.
The L1 penalty (Lasso) introduces a constraint that drives uninformative coefficients exactly to zero, automatically performing variable selection.
The scoring metric used for tuning was ROC-AUC, chosen for its robustness to class imbalance and its relevance in directional classification tasks.

Final embedded feature set (from L1):
'vix_level', 'snp500_ret_1', 'qqq_rsi_20', 'risk_aversion_proxy', 'tlt_ret_5', 'qqq_vol_20', 'qqq_vol_z', 'qqq_logret_1', 'qqq_lb_5', 'qqq_volume', 'qqq_atr_20'

Observation.

  • Compact yet diverse subset.
    The L1 step reduced the 25 RFE-selected features down to 11 non-zero coefficients, yielding a lean but multi-dimensional representation that balances volatility, momentum, and cross-asset context.
  • Volatility and risk sentiment dominate.
    vix_level emerged as the strongest positive coefficient, confirming that elevated implied volatility is a key driver of downside market risk.
    qqq_vol_20 and qqq_vol_z further emphasize realized and volume-based volatility as important short-term predictors.
  • Macro risk relationships preserved.
    The inclusion of risk_aversion_proxy (difference between VIX and TLT log-returns) and tlt_ret_5 reflects sensitivity to broader risk-off dynamics in fixed income and volatility markets.
    These terms capture the interplay between equity fear (VIX) and bond demand (TLT).
  • Market-wide co-movement retained.
    snp500_ret_1 remained with a negative coefficient, suggesting that a positive move in the broad S&P 500 tends to align inversely with the binary “up” signal once other volatility variables are controlled—consistent with overreaction or mean-reversion tendencies at very short horizons.
  • Momentum and range effects modest.
    qqq_logret_1, qqq_lb_5, and qqq_rsi_20 contributed smaller but meaningful coefficients, representing local momentum and oversold/overbought behavior.
    qqq_atr_20 adds a volatility-of-range measure, capturing regime changes in daily amplitude.
  • Reduced redundancy, improved interpretability.
    The L1 process pruned redundant moving-average and level variables from RFE, highlighting orthogonal drivers rather than overlapping technicals.
    The result is a concise, economically interpretable feature set spanning volatility, risk sentiment, and short-term momentum.

Table B4 (L1 coefficients by |coef|).

feature coef abs_coef selected_nonzero
0 vix_level 0.3566 0.3566 True
1 snp500_ret_1 -0.2282 0.2282 True
2 qqq_rsi_20 -0.1794 0.1794 True
3 risk_aversion_proxy -0.1651 0.1651 True
4 tlt_ret_5 0.1182 0.1182 True
5 qqq_vol_20 -0.1023 0.1023 True
6 qqq_vol_z 0.0574 0.0574 True
7 qqq_logret_1 0.0518 0.0518 True
8 qqq_lb_5 0.046 0.046 True
9 qqq_volume -0.0407 0.0407 True
10 qqq_atr_20 0.0268 0.0268 True
11 qqq_sma_5 0.0 0.0 False
12 qqq_open 0.0 0.0 False
13 qqq_ema_20 0.0 0.0 False
14 qqq_low 0.0 0.0 False
15 qqq_sma_20 0.0 0.0 False
16 qqq_ub_5 0.0 0.0 False
17 snp500_level 0.0 0.0 False
18 qqq_high 0.0 0.0 False
19 qqq_ema_5 0.0 0.0 False

Figure B4.1 — Absolute Coefficients of Selected Features (L1 Regularization)

As part of the comparative exercise XGBoost classifier was also tested but finally the outputs from L1 Regulatization were used for further analysis.

Estimator. XGBClassifier with max_depth of 3. Final embedded feature set (from XGBClassifier):
'vix_level', 'qqq_ema_5', 'qqq_logret_1', 'qqq_sma_5', 'qqq_ub_5', 'snp500_ret_1', 'qqq_sma_20', 'qqq_lb_5', 'tlt_ret_5', 'risk_aversion_proxy', 'qqq_rsi_20', 'qqq_lb_20'

B.5 Funnel Summary (Filter → Wrapper → Embedded)¶

Stage Technique Kept Examples Purpose
Filter Mutual Information 40 qqq_ret_1, vix_logret_1, vol_10 Remove obvious noise
Wrapper RFE (LogReg) 25 'qqq_ema_20', 'vix_level', 'qqq_lb_5', 'qqq_sma_20', 'qqq_open' Remove redundancy under linearity
Embed L1 (LogRegCV, TSCV) 11 'vix_level', 'snp500_ret_1', 'qqq_rsi_20', 'risk_aversion_proxy' Enforce sparsity & stability

Takeaway. The final embedded set preserves short-horizon momentum/volatility information and cross-asset risk signals while controlling redundancy. This balances parsimony and predictive content for the SVM in Part C.

Part C — Model Building, Tuning, and Evaluation¶

C.1 Objective & Setup¶

We train a Support Vector Machine (SVM) classifier to predict positive next-day QQQ moves (threshold +0.50%) using the final embedded feature subset from Part B. A Support Vector Machine (SVM) classifier is suitable for high-dimensional feature spaces due to its ability to construct non-linear decision boundaries through kernel transformations. Unlike linear models, which assume a direct relationship between predictors and the target, the SVM can effectively separate complex market states by maximizing the margin between classes (up/down moves).

Given the heterogeneity of financial indicators (momentum, volatility, cross-asset), a non-linear boundary is conceptually justified — the relationships among features are rarely additive or monotonic.

Pipeline. SimpleImputer(median) → StandardScaler → SVC(probability=True, class_weight='balanced')
CV. 5-fold TimeSeriesSplit to respect chronology.
Train/Test split. Chronological, last ≈ 20% reserved for test.
Horizon & Target. Next-day return; y=1 if $`r_{t+1}>0.5%`$.

C.2 Hyperparameter Tuning¶

Grid.

  • kernel ∈ {rbf, poly}
  • C ∈ {0.5, 1, 2, 5, 10}
  • gamma ∈ {scale, 0.1, 0.05, 0.01}
  • degree ∈ {2,3} (for poly)

Scoring. primary = ROC-AUC, with secondary tracking of F1 and Accuracy.

Best CV result.

  • Best params: {'clf__C': 0.5, 'clf__degree': 2, 'clf__gamma': 0.01, 'clf__kernel': 'rbf'}
  • Best CV ROC-AUC: 0.605

C.3 Test-Set Performance¶

Using the best cross-validated SVM on the held-out test window:

Metric Value Interpretation
ROC-AUC: 0.6129 Overall ranking ability (0.5 = random, 1.0 = perfect)
Accuracy: 0.5419 Correct directional classification rate
Precision: 0.3861 Probability of a correct “up” prediction
Recall: 0.6595 Coverage of actual positive days
F1-Score: 0.487 Harmonic mean of precision and recall

Confusion Matrix.

ROC Curve.

Classification Report.

Key Insights:

  • The model achieved an ROC-AUC modestly above random (≈ 0.55–0.60), which is typical for daily directional forecasts given the low signal-to-noise ratio in financial returns.
  • Precision and recall trade-offs indicate that predicted “up” days tend to have moderate success but high false-positive risk when volatility regimes shift.
  • The confusion matrix reveals near-symmetric misclassification rates, implying limited but consistent discriminative power rather than systematic bias toward one class.
  • Performance generalization remained stable across folds, suggesting that the chosen regularization (C, $\gamma$) successfully avoided overfitting.

Rationale of Results and Economic Interpretation

  1. Why SVM performs as expected:
    Market returns are largely driven by random noise and regime transitions, which produce overlapping class boundaries.
    The SVM’s margin-maximization allows it to extract small, stable structural patterns (e.g., volatility clustering, short-term momentum) while avoiding high-variance fits.

  2. Why RBF kernel outperforms linear models:
    Linear separation assumes additive feature effects, which is unrealistic when volatility and cross-asset interactions have conditional, state-dependent influence.
    The RBF kernel adapts to these nonlinearities by projecting features into a higher-dimensional space where subtle curvature in decision boundaries can emerge.

  3. Economic interpretation of coefficients:
    Because SVMs are non-parametric, feature influence was interpreted through permutation importance.
    Volatility-related predictors (vix_level, qqq_vol_20, qqq_vol_z) and cross-asset risk proxies (tlt_ret_5, risk_aversion_proxy) showed the highest importance, confirming that risk sentiment and volatility regimes dominate short-term price direction.

  4. Realistic expectations:
    The modest test performance aligns with academic literature: daily returns exhibit weak predictability.
    Predictive signals exist but are small, time-varying, and often dominated by transaction costs or regime shifts.
    The trained SVM thus provides informational value for probabilistic forecasting or risk-filtering rather than a standalone trading edge.

C.4 Robustness & Sensitivity¶

  • Threshold sensitivity. If the +0.50% threshold is raised/lowered, if the class balance changes notably; performance may shift accordingly.
  • Regime effects. Macro cycles (e.g., 2020–2022) shift vol and correlation structure; periodic re-training or rolling windows are advisable.

C.5 Summary Model Building, Tuning, and Evaluation¶

  • The feature funnel produced a compact, interpretable set dominated by momentum, volatility, and risk-on/off signals.
  • The SVM (RBF) strikes a balance between flexibility and control, allowing for nonlinear decision surfaces without excessive parameterization . The model achieved 0.6129 AUC on the test set—directionally informative but limited for standalone trading.
  • These results are realistic for daily equity direction prediction and set the stage for risk-aware strategy tests.
  • Hyperparameter tuning with TimeSeriesSplit ensured realistic evaluation and prevented temporal leakage.
  • Future refinements could include ensemble averaging (SVM + XGBoost), rolling retraining, or feature-set expansion to macro variables for improved regime adaptability.

Trading Strategy — Summary & Conclusions¶

Overview¶

A simple directional trading strategy was implemented based on the SVM model’s predicted probabilities of positive next-day returns for the QQQ ETF.
The backtest covers the period 2015-01-01 to 2025-09-30, with an in-sample (training) phase from 2015–2023 and an out-of-sample (live) evaluation covering 2023–2025.
Trading logic: Enter a long position when the model predicts y = 1 (uptrend probability > 0.5), and hold cash otherwise.

All results below are reported on a daily frequency, net of transaction costs assumed negligible for illustration.

Backtest Performance Summary¶

Metric Backtest (All) In-Sample Out-of-Sample
Annual return 8.69% 5.07% 24.43%
Cumulative return 152.7% 55.36% 62.67%
Annual volatility 19.1% 19.5% 17.6%
Sharpe ratio 0.53 0.35 1.33
Calmar ratio 0.23 0.14 1.03
Max drawdown -37.2% -37.2% -23.8%
Sortino ratio 0.74 0.48 2.01
Omega ratio 1.15 1.09 1.40
Beta (vs QQQ) 0.78 0.78 0.78
Alpha -0.03 -0.05 0.06
Daily VaR (95%) -2.37% -2.43% -2.13%

Key Observations and Insights¶

  1. Return and Risk Characteristics
  • The strategy achieved a total cumulative gain of +152% over the 10.7-year period, corresponding to an annualized return of 8.7% and annual volatility of 19%.
  • The Sharpe ratio of 0.53 reflects modest risk-adjusted performance. Out-of-sample Sharpe improved to 1.33, suggesting better calibration of model probabilities in live data.
  • Despite acceptable risk metrics, the strategy underperformed a simple buy-and-hold investment in QQQ, which delivered a substantially higher cumulative and annual return over the same horizon. This indicates that while the model occasionally sidestepped large drawdowns, it also missed major bull-market phases when the model stayed flat due to weak uptrend signals.
  1. Comparison to Buy-and-Hold Benchmark
  • Buy-and-Hold (QQQ): ~17–18% annualized return (approx.) over the same 2015–2025 period.
  • Model Strategy: 8.7% annualized return with lower beta (≈0.78).
  • Interpretation: The model’s cautious, binary exposure led to lower volatility but also lower participation in prolonged upward markets (2016–2021, 2023–2025). The reduced upside capture offset any risk savings, resulting in negative alpha (−0.03) relative to the benchmark.
  • In essence, the strategy acts more as a risk-managed derivative of the index—smoother returns, smaller drawdowns, but at the cost of lagging total performance.
  1. In-Sample vs. Out-of-Sample Behavior
  • The in-sample phase produced conservative results (5% annual return, −37% max drawdown).
  • The out-of-sample period (2023–2025) saw stronger returns (+24% annualized) and improved Sharpe (1.33), showing some adaptability but still below passive benchmark levels.
  1. Drawdowns and Risk Exposure
  • The largest drawdowns (−37% and −34%) occurred during 2018–2020 and 2021–2022, coinciding with market shocks.
  • Although the model reduced exposure during some volatile regimes, it failed to re-enter quickly, prolonging recovery times versus buy-and-hold.
  1. Rolling Metrics
  • Rolling Sharpe ratios hovered near 0.5 overall, occasionally spiking above 2.0 during stable regimes.
  • Rolling beta (~0.78) confirms that the strategy participated in equity risk but with incomplete upside capture.
  • Volatility control was effective—realized volatility remained below QQQ’s—but not sufficient to offset the opportunity cost of missed rallies.
  1. Distribution and Seasonality
  • Monthly returns were more stable and less extreme than QQQ’s, but with smaller positive outliers.
  • Performance improved seasonally toward year-end (Nov–Dec), consistent with typical QQQ cyclicality, yet remained below the index’s total return path.

Interpretation and Strategic Assessment¶

  • Predictive validity: The model provides a weak but exploitable probabilistic edge consistent with low signal-to-noise expectations in equity - When benchmarked against a passive long-only QQQ position, the model demonstrates better downside control but weaker upside participation.
  • The lower volatility and drawdowns are evidence of improved risk management, yet the alpha contribution is negative, confirming no excess return after adjusting for risk.
  • Economically, this reflects the inherent limitation of daily-horizon ML forecasts: avoiding bad days is statistically harder than missing good days.
  • From a portfolio standpoint, the model could still be valuable as a tactical overlay or volatility-reduction component, but not as a standalone outperforming strategy.

Overall Summary¶

The backtest confirms that while the SVM-based signal provides modest probabilistic insight into short-term direction, its practical utility lies in risk modulation, not return enhancement.
The model may serve as a useful exposure-scaling or hedging layer within a diversified allocation, particularly for reducing drawdowns during volatility spikes, but it does not outperform buy-and-hold when measured on a pure return basis.

Python Code¶

Import Libraries

In [1]:
# Imports + load
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
from pathlib import Path
import os
import sys
from tabulate import tabulate


# plotting + metrics
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from sklearn.metrics import (
    accuracy_score, precision_score, recall_score, f1_score,
    roc_auc_score, confusion_matrix, classification_report, ConfusionMatrixDisplay,
    RocCurveDisplay, precision_recall_curve, PrecisionRecallDisplay, roc_curve
)


# Sklearn feature selection
from sklearn.feature_selection import mutual_info_classif
from sklearn.impute import SimpleImputer
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
from sklearn.feature_selection import RFE
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegressionCV
from sklearn.model_selection import (train_test_split,
                                    RandomizedSearchCV,
                                    TimeSeriesSplit,
                                    cross_val_score, 
                                    GridSearchCV
                                    )


# modeling
from sklearn.pipeline import Pipeline
from sklearn.svm import SVC

# optional: for permutation importance
from sklearn.inspection import permutation_importance

# portfolio testing of strategy
if not hasattr(np, 'NINF'):
    np.NINF = -np.inf

# Import pyfolio
import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings('ignore')
import pyfolio as pf

# Quantmod
import quantmod.charts
from quantmod.timeseries import dailyReturn
from quantmod.indicators import BBands

Initial Setup¶

I1.) Configuration¶

  • Set your data folder, main ticker key (derived from filename prefix like qqq_raw.csv → qqq), date range, and labeling parameters.
  • Outputs will be written to ./datasets/.
In [2]:
# Configuration
DATA_DIR = Path('./data')  # folder with qqq_raw.csv, CrudeOil_raw.csv, Gold_raw.csv, tlt_raw.csv, etc.
MAIN_TICKER = 'qqq'        # must match the file prefix before _raw.csv, lowercased
START = '2005-01-01'       # optional; set None to use full range
END   = '2025-10-01'       # optional; set None to use full range
HORIZON = 1                # days ahead for label
THRESHOLD = 0.005          # 0.5% up move = positive class

OUT_DIR = Path('./datasets'); OUT_DIR.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
OUT_CSV = OUT_DIR / 'combined_dataset.csv'
OUT_PARQUET = OUT_DIR / 'combined_dataset.parquet'
print('Config OK', DATA_DIR, MAIN_TICKER, START, END)

Config OK data qqq 2005-01-01 2025-10-01

I2.) Utility Functions¶

Reusable helpers for cleaning prices, indicators, feature engineering, prefixing, joining, and target labeling.

In [3]:
# Ensure DataFrame has a DatetimeIndex
def ensure_dt_index(df: pd.DataFrame) -> pd.DataFrame:
    if not isinstance(df.index, pd.DatetimeIndex):
        if 'Date' in df.columns:
            df = df.set_index('Date')
        elif 'date' in df.columns:
            df = df.set_index('date')
        else:
            raise ValueError("DataFrame must have a DatetimeIndex or a 'Date'/'date' column.")
    return df.sort_index()

# Clean price DataFrame: ensure datetime index, sort, remove duplicates, reindex to business days
def clean_prices(df: pd.DataFrame) -> pd.DataFrame:
    df = df.copy()
    # Ensure Date index, then PARSE as datetime with dayfirst=True (matches your CSVs)
    if not isinstance(df.index, pd.DatetimeIndex):
        if 'Date' in df.columns:
            df = df.set_index('Date')
        elif 'date' in df.columns:
            df = df.set_index('date')
        else:
            raise ValueError("DataFrame must have a DatetimeIndex or a 'Date'/'date' column.")
    df.index = pd.to_datetime(df.index, dayfirst=True, errors='coerce')
    df = df.sort_index()
    df = df[~df.index.duplicated(keep='last')]
    if df.index.hasnans:
        df = df[~df.index.isna()]

    if len(df) == 0:
        return df

    # Reindex to business days; forward-fill OHLC, zero-fill Volume
    idx = pd.bdate_range(df.index.min(), df.index.max())
    df = df.reindex(idx)
    for col in ['Open','High','Low','Close']:
        if col in df.columns:
            df[col] = df[col].ffill()
    if 'Volume' in df.columns:
        df['Volume'] = df['Volume'].fillna(0)
    return df

# Optional internal Bollinger fallback (used only if BBands isn't available)
def _bollinger_fallback(close: pd.Series, window: int = 20, n_std: float = 2.0):
    ma = close.rolling(window).mean()
    sd = close.rolling(window).std()
    upper = ma + n_std * sd
    lower = ma - n_std * sd
    return lower, ma, upper

# RSI calculation
def rsi(close: pd.Series, period: int = 14) -> pd.Series:
    delta = close.diff()
    gain = delta.where(delta > 0, 0.0).rolling(period).mean()
    loss = (-delta.where(delta < 0, 0.0)).rolling(period).mean()
    rs = gain / loss.replace(0, np.nan)
    return 100 - (100 / (1 + rs))

# True Range calculation
def true_range(high: pd.Series, low: pd.Series, prev_close: pd.Series) -> pd.Series:
    a = (high - low).abs()
    b = (high - prev_close).abs()
    c = (low - prev_close).abs()
    return pd.concat([a, b, c], axis=1).max(axis=1)

# Creating features for QQQ, main ticker of interest
def compute_features_main(df: pd.DataFrame) -> pd.DataFrame:
    df = ensure_dt_index(df.copy())
    close = df['Close']
    # Returns
    df['ret_1'] = close.pct_change()
    df['ret_5'] = close.pct_change(5)
    df['logret_1'] = np.log(close).diff()
    # EMAs / SMAs
    df['ema_5'] = close.ewm(span=5, adjust=False).mean()
    df['ema_20'] = close.ewm(span=20, adjust=False).mean()
    df['ema_ratio_5_20'] = df['ema_5'] / df['ema_20']
    df['sma_5'] = close.rolling(5).mean()
    df['sma_20'] = close.rolling(20).mean()
    df['sma_ratio_5_20'] = df['sma_5'] / df['sma_20']
    # Volatility
    df['vol_10'] = df['ret_1'].rolling(10).std()
    df['vol_20'] = df['ret_1'].rolling(20).std()
    # ATR
    if {'High','Low','Close'}.issubset(df.columns):
        tr = true_range(df['High'], df['Low'], close.shift(1))
        df['atr_5'] = tr.rolling(5).mean()
        df['atr_20'] = tr.rolling(20).mean()
    # Volume z-score
    if 'Volume' in df.columns:
        vmean = df['Volume'].rolling(20).mean()
        vstd  = df['Volume'].rolling(20).std()
        df['vol_z'] = (df['Volume'] - vmean) / vstd
    # RSI + Bollinger (use BBands if available; else fallback)
    for period in [5, 20]:
        df[f'rsi_{period}'] = rsi(close, period)
        try:
            lb, mb, ub = BBands(close, n=period, ndev=2)  # quantmod-style
            df[f'LB_{period}'] = lb
            df[f'UB_{period}'] = ub
        except Exception:
            # Safe fallback if BBands isn't defined in this environment
            lb, ma, ub = _bollinger_fallback(close, window=period, n_std=2.0)
            df[f'LB_{period}'] = lb
            df[f'UB_{period}'] = ub
    return df

# Creating features for the other cross asset tickers
def compute_features_cross(df: pd.DataFrame) -> pd.DataFrame:
    df = ensure_dt_index(df.copy())
    close = df['Close']
    out = pd.DataFrame(index=df.index)
    out['ret_1'] = close.pct_change()
    out['ret_5'] = close.pct_change(5)
    out['logret_1'] = np.log(close).diff()
    out['level'] = close
    return out

# Prefix all columns in df with given prefix (lowercased)
def prefix_columns(df: pd.DataFrame, prefix: str) -> pd.DataFrame:
    return df.rename(columns={c: f"{prefix}_{c.lower()}" for c in df.columns})

# Build labels based on main ticker future returns
def build_labels_from_main(df: pd.DataFrame, main_ticker: str, horizon: int = 1, threshold: float = 0.0025) -> pd.DataFrame:
    main_close = f"{main_ticker}_close"
    if main_close not in df.columns:
        raise ValueError(f"Main ticker close not found: {main_close}")
    future_ret = df[main_close].pct_change(periods=horizon).shift(-horizon)
    df['target_ret'] = future_ret
    df['y'] = np.where(future_ret > threshold, 1, 0)
    return df

I3.) Load Raw CSVs, apply feature rules, prefix, and collect frames¶

Processing the raw inputs to load the data, apply features to our main ticker (qqq) and cross asset features for model building.

In [4]:
frames, keys = [], []
raw_files = sorted(DATA_DIR.glob('*_raw.csv'))
if not raw_files:
    raise FileNotFoundError(f'No *_raw.csv in {DATA_DIR.resolve()}')

for f in raw_files:
    key = f.stem.replace('_raw','').lower()
    df = pd.read_csv(f)
    df = clean_prices(df)
    if START is not None:
        df = df.loc[df.index >= pd.to_datetime(START)]
    if END is not None:
        df = df.loc[df.index <= pd.to_datetime(END)]
    if key == MAIN_TICKER:
        feat = compute_features_main(df)
    else:
        feat = compute_features_cross(df)
    feat_pref = prefix_columns(feat, key)
    frames.append(feat_pref)
    keys.append(key)
    print(f'Processed {key}:', feat_pref.shape)

Processed crudeoil: (5412, 4)
Processed gold: (5412, 4)
Processed qqq: (5412, 25)
Processed snp500: (5412, 4)
Processed tlt: (5412, 4)
Processed vix: (5412, 4)

I4.) Join & drop initial NaNs¶

Data cleaning

In [5]:
import pandas as pd
combined = pd.concat(frames, axis=1, join='outer').sort_index()
combined = combined.ffill().bfill(limit=1)

# Drop columns that are entirely NaN
all_nan_cols = combined.columns[combined.isna().all()]
if len(all_nan_cols):
    print('Dropping all-NaN columns:', len(all_nan_cols))
    combined = combined.drop(columns=all_nan_cols)

# Require minimal main ticker columns present
required_main = [f'{MAIN_TICKER}_close', f'{MAIN_TICKER}_ret_1', f'{MAIN_TICKER}_ret_5']
required_main = [c for c in required_main if c in combined.columns]
mask = combined[required_main].notna().all(axis=1)
combined = combined.loc[mask]
print('Combined shape:', combined.shape)

Combined shape: (5408, 45)

I5.) Detect keys and add composite features¶

Creating some additonal cross asset features for our model.

In [6]:
def find_key(cands):
    for k in keys:
        for c in cands:
            if c in k:
                return k
    return None

spx_key   = find_key(['sp500', 'snp500','spx'])
vix_key   = find_key(['vix'])
tlt_key   = find_key(['tlt'])
crude_key = find_key(['crude','oil'])
gold_key  = find_key(['gold'])
print({'spx':spx_key,'vix':vix_key,'tlt':tlt_key,'crude':crude_key,'gold':gold_key})

if spx_key is not None:
    combined['rel_strength_nasdaq_spx'] = combined.get(f'{MAIN_TICKER}_ret_5') - combined.get(f'{spx_key}_ret_5')
if (vix_key is not None) and (tlt_key is not None):
    combined['risk_aversion_proxy'] = combined.get(f'{vix_key}_logret_1') - combined.get(f'{tlt_key}_logret_1')
if (crude_key is not None) and (gold_key is not None):
    combined['inflation_beta'] = combined.get(f'{crude_key}_ret_5') - combined.get(f'{gold_key}_ret_5')


combined[['rel_strength_nasdaq_spx','risk_aversion_proxy','inflation_beta']].dropna()
print('Combined shape:', combined.shape)

{'spx': 'snp500', 'vix': 'vix', 'tlt': 'tlt', 'crude': 'crudeoil', 'gold': 'gold'}
Combined shape: (5408, 48)

I6.) Labeling of Main indicator for QQQ future return¶

Creating labels for model training and testing, checking the distribution of target label in our dataset.

In [7]:
# Build labels and show class balance
combined = build_labels_from_main(combined, MAIN_TICKER, HORIZON, THRESHOLD)
print('Class balance (y=1):', combined['y'].mean().round(3))

# Pie Plot
combined['y'].value_counts().iplot(kind='pie', title='Binary Classes')

Class balance (y=1): 0.32

7.) Save dataset¶

In [8]:
OUT_DIR = Path('./datasets'); OUT_DIR.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
combined.to_csv(OUT_DIR / 'combined_dataset_patched.csv')
combined.to_parquet(OUT_DIR / 'combined_dataset_patched.parquet')
print('Saved:', OUT_DIR / 'combined_dataset_patched.csv')
print('Saved:', OUT_DIR / 'combined_dataset_patched.parquet')

Saved: datasets\combined_dataset_patched.csv
Saved: datasets\combined_dataset_patched.parquet

8.) Sanity checks on the final dataset by ploting some final metrics¶

In [9]:
plt.figure()
plt.plot(combined.index, combined[f'{MAIN_TICKER}_close'])
plt.title(f'{MAIN_TICKER.upper()} Close')
plt.show()

# Drop label from dataframe
combined.drop('y', axis=1, inplace=True)

# Plot correlation matrix
combined.corr().iplot(
    kind="heatmap",
    title="Features Set Correlations"
    )

Part B¶

Feature Selection Using the Funnelling Approach
Perform feature selection for a machine learning model using a multi-step process by combining techniques from lter, wrapper, and embedded methods.

B1.) Load the patched dataset¶

Configuration of our sample for Modelling and evaluation

In [10]:
# Load patched dataset

DATASET_DIR = Path("./datasets")
PARQ = DATASET_DIR / "combined_dataset_patched.parquet"
CSV  = DATASET_DIR / "combined_dataset_patched.csv"
SAMPLE_START = '2015-01-01'

if PARQ.exists():
    df = pd.read_parquet(PARQ)
else:
    df = pd.read_csv(CSV, parse_dates=["Date"]).set_index("Date")

df = df[df.index >= SAMPLE_START].copy()

print(df.shape)

(2804, 50)

B2.) Checking data¶

Labels from QQQ future return

In [11]:
# Distribution of target variable
print('Class balance (y=1):', df['y'].mean().round(3))
# Pie Plot
df['y'].value_counts().iplot(kind='pie', title='Binary Classes')

Class balance (y=1): 0.326

B3.) Define features, target variable and some basic sanity checks¶

In [12]:
# 2) Define X / y
TARGET_COL = "y"
LEAKY_COLS = ["target_ret"]  # contains the future return; must not be in X

assert TARGET_COL in df.columns, "y not found. Re-run labeling cell earlier."

X = df.drop(columns=[c for c in [TARGET_COL] + LEAKY_COLS if c in df.columns]).copy()
y = df[TARGET_COL].astype(int).copy()

# quick class balance
print("Class balance (y=1):", y.mean().round(3))
print("Total features:", X.shape[1])

# optional: drop constant or near-constant columns (rare but safe)
nunique = X.nunique()
const_cols = nunique[nunique <= 1].index.tolist()
if const_cols:
    print("Dropping constant columns:", len(const_cols))
    X = X.drop(columns=const_cols)

print("Features after dropping constant cols:", X.shape[1])
# Quick overview of features
print("Feature overview:")
print(tabulate(X.describe().T.round(3), headers='keys', tablefmt='pretty'))

Plotting the correlations among feature and Close prices of the Main Ticker¶
In [13]:
# Plot main ticker close price
plt.figure()
plt.plot(X.index, X[f'{MAIN_TICKER}_close'])
plt.title(f'{MAIN_TICKER.upper()} Close')
plt.show()

# Drop label from dataframe
#df.drop('y', axis=1, inplace=True)

# Plot correlation matrix
X.corr().iplot(
    kind="heatmap",
    title="Features Set Correlations"
    )

B4.) Feature Selection Using the Funnelling Approach¶

We’ll use a three-stage funnel:

  • Filter methods (statistical relevance)

  • Wrapper methods (recursive model-based selection)

  • Embedded methods (regularization-based importance)

Filter method
Mutual Information captures non-linear dependencies between each feature and the target, keeping the top predictors while discarding noise.

In [14]:
# Filter Step: Univariate feature selection using Mutual Information

# 1) Mutual Information between each feature and target
mi = mutual_info_classif(X.fillna(0), y, random_state=42)
mi_series = pd.Series(mi, index=X.columns).sort_values(ascending=False)

# Keep top 40 as first funnel cut (adjust if many features)
top_filter = mi_series.head(40).index.tolist()

print("Filter features top 10:")
print(mi_series.head(10)) 
# print(top_filter)
print("Selected features after filter step:", len(top_filter))

# Convert to DataFrame for easy plotting
mi_df = mi_series.head(20).sort_values(ascending=True)  # ascending for horizontal bars

plt.figure(figsize=(8,10))
plt.barh(mi_df.index, mi_df.values, color='steelblue')
plt.title("Top 20 Features Ranked by Mutual Information", fontsize=13)
plt.xlabel("Mutual Information Score")
plt.ylabel("Feature")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Filter features top 10:
qqq_ema_20      0.027300
vix_level       0.027105
qqq_vol_10      0.025090
qqq_lb_5        0.022818
vix_ret_5       0.021009
qqq_sma_20      0.018263
qqq_open        0.016567
qqq_sma_5       0.015848
snp500_ret_5    0.015134
tlt_level       0.013611
dtype: float64
Selected features after filter step: 40

Wrapper Step
RFE recursively eliminates least-important coefficients using a linear estimator, leaving a compact subset with good discrimination power.

In [15]:
# Wrapper Step: Recursive Feature Elimination (RFE) with Logistic Regression

Xr = X[top_filter].copy()
Xr = Xr.replace([np.inf, -np.inf], np.nan)

# nan_counts = Xr.isna().sum().sort_values(ascending=False)
# print("Top features with NaNs:\n", nan_counts.head(10))
# print("Total NaN cells:", int(Xr.isna().sum().sum()))

# median impute -> scale; keep DataFrame to preserve column names
imp = SimpleImputer(strategy="median")
Xr_i = pd.DataFrame(imp.fit_transform(Xr), index=Xr.index, columns=Xr.columns)

scaler = StandardScaler(with_mean=True, with_std=True)
Xr_s = pd.DataFrame(scaler.fit_transform(Xr_i), index=Xr_i.index, columns=Xr_i.columns)

# sanity check
assert not np.isnan(Xr_s.values).any(), "Still have NaNs after imputation."
print("Preprocessed shape:", Xr_s.shape)

Preprocessed shape: (2804, 40)
In [16]:
# Select up to 25 features or total-1 if fewer using RFE with Logistic Regression
n_select = min(25, max(1, Xr_s.shape[1] - 1))

wrapper_model = LogisticRegression(
    max_iter=200,
    solver='liblinear',
    class_weight='balanced',
    random_state=42
)

rfe = RFE(estimator=wrapper_model, n_features_to_select=n_select, step=1)
rfe.fit(Xr_s, y)

rfe_features = Xr_s.columns[rfe.support_].tolist()
print("Wrapper-selected features:", rfe_features)
print("Number of wrapper-selected features:", len(rfe_features))

Wrapper-selected features: ['qqq_ema_20', 'vix_level', 'qqq_lb_5', 'qqq_sma_20', 'qqq_open', 'qqq_sma_5', 'qqq_low', 'qqq_atr_20', 'qqq_volume', 'qqq_vol_20', 'vix_ret_1', 'qqq_ub_5', 'vix_logret_1', 'qqq_ema_5', 'qqq_high', 'snp500_level', 'qqq_rsi_20', 'qqq_lb_20', 'qqq_ret_1', 'qqq_logret_1', 'qqq_vol_z', 'snp500_ret_1', 'tlt_ret_5', 'qqq_ub_20', 'risk_aversion_proxy']
Number of wrapper-selected features: 25
In [17]:
# RFE ranking table
rfe_table = (
    pd.DataFrame({
        "feature": Xr_s.columns,
        "rfe_rank": rfe.ranking_,
        "selected": rfe.support_
    })
    .sort_values(["rfe_rank", "feature"])
    .reset_index(drop=True)
)

rfe_ranked_list = rfe_table.sort_values("rfe_rank")[["feature","rfe_rank"]].values.tolist()
rfe_ranked_list[:5]
Out[17]:
[['qqq_atr_20', 1],
 ['qqq_ema_20', 1],
 ['qqq_ema_5', 1],
 ['qqq_high', 1],
 ['qqq_lb_20', 1]]

Embedded step (L1 regularization)
Embedded methods evaluate features during model training; L1 regularization enforces sparsity, while gradient boosting captures non-linear effects. In the current setup we are using the L1 regularization.

In [18]:
# L1 with wider C grid + time-series CV + F1 scoring

# Split the data set using TimeSeriesSplit
tscv = TimeSeriesSplit(n_splits=5)
Cs = np.logspace(-4, 2, 30)  # much wider than default

embed = LogisticRegressionCV(
    Cs=Cs,
    penalty='l1',
    solver='liblinear',            # L1 ok
    class_weight='balanced',
    scoring='roc_auc',                  # or 'roc_auc' or 'f1'
    cv=tscv,
    max_iter=200,
    n_jobs=-1,
    refit=True
)
embed.fit(Xr_s[rfe_features], y)

coef = pd.Series(np.abs(embed.coef_[0]), index=rfe_features)
final_features = coef[coef > 0].sort_values(ascending=False).index.tolist()
print("Final embedded-selected features:", final_features)
coef.sort_values(ascending=False).head(15)
print("No of final features using L1 regularization with time-series CV: ", len(final_features))

# Plot correlation matrix of final features
X_final = Xr_s[final_features].copy()
X_final.corr().iplot(
    kind="heatmap",
    title="Final Selected Features Correlations"
    )

Final embedded-selected features: ['vix_level', 'snp500_ret_1', 'qqq_rsi_20', 'risk_aversion_proxy', 'tlt_ret_5', 'qqq_vol_20', 'qqq_vol_z', 'qqq_logret_1', 'qqq_lb_5', 'qqq_volume', 'qqq_atr_20']
No of final features using L1 regularization with time-series CV:  11
In [19]:
# Coefficients for the L1-CV model (per feature in the RFE subset)
coefs = embed.coef_[0]                   # raw coefficients
abs_coefs = np.abs(coefs)
keep_mask = abs_coefs > 0

l1_table = (
    pd.DataFrame({
        "feature": rfe_features,
        "coef": coefs,
        "abs_coef": abs_coefs,
        "selected_nonzero": keep_mask
    })
    .sort_values("abs_coef", ascending=False)
    .reset_index(drop=True)
)

# Best C chosen by CV (per-class array; binary -> shape (1,))
best_C = float(np.atleast_1d(embed.C_)[0])

print("Best C (L1-CV):", best_C)
print("Non-zero features (L1):", int(keep_mask.sum()))

# Save + peek
# l1_table.to_csv(ART / "B4_L1_abscoef.csv", index=False)
# print("Saved L1 coefficients to:", ART / "B4_L1_abscoef.csv")
# l1_table.head(20)
print(tabulate(l1_table.head(20).round(4), headers='keys', tablefmt='pretty'))

Best C (L1-CV): 0.20433597178569418
Non-zero features (L1): 11
+----+---------------------+---------+----------+------------------+
|    |       feature       |  coef   | abs_coef | selected_nonzero |
+----+---------------------+---------+----------+------------------+
| 0  |      vix_level      | 0.3566  |  0.3566  |       True       |
| 1  |    snp500_ret_1     | -0.2283 |  0.2283  |       True       |
| 2  |     qqq_rsi_20      | -0.1794 |  0.1794  |       True       |
| 3  | risk_aversion_proxy | -0.1651 |  0.1651  |       True       |
| 4  |      tlt_ret_5      | 0.1182  |  0.1182  |       True       |
| 5  |     qqq_vol_20      | -0.102  |  0.102   |       True       |
| 6  |      qqq_vol_z      | 0.0574  |  0.0574  |       True       |
| 7  |    qqq_logret_1     | 0.0519  |  0.0519  |       True       |
| 8  |      qqq_lb_5       | 0.0463  |  0.0463  |       True       |
| 9  |     qqq_volume      | -0.0408 |  0.0408  |       True       |
| 10 |     qqq_atr_20      | 0.0264  |  0.0264  |       True       |
| 11 |      qqq_sma_5      |   0.0   |   0.0    |      False       |
| 12 |      qqq_open       |   0.0   |   0.0    |      False       |
| 13 |     qqq_ema_20      |   0.0   |   0.0    |      False       |
| 14 |       qqq_low       |   0.0   |   0.0    |      False       |
| 15 |     qqq_sma_20      |   0.0   |   0.0    |      False       |
| 16 |      qqq_ub_5       |   0.0   |   0.0    |      False       |
| 17 |    snp500_level     |   0.0   |   0.0    |      False       |
| 18 |      qqq_high       |   0.0   |   0.0    |      False       |

In [20]:
final_features = l1_table.loc[l1_table["selected_nonzero"], "feature"].tolist()
len(final_features), final_features[:10]

K = 15
topK = l1_table.head(K).sort_values("abs_coef", ascending=True)

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
plt.figure(figsize=(6, 6))
plt.barh(topK["feature"], topK["abs_coef"])
plt.title(f"Top {K} L1 Coefficients (|coef|)")
plt.xlabel("|coef|")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

# Optional: XGBoost embedded feature importance from xgboost import XGBClassifier import pandas as pd xgb = XGBClassifier( n_estimators=300, max_depth=3, learning_rate=0.05, subsample=0.8, colsample_bytree=0.8, random_state=42, n_jobs=-1 ) xgb.fit(Xr_s[rfe_features], y) imp = pd.Series(xgb.feature_importances_, index=rfe_features).sort_values(ascending=False) final_features = imp.head(12).index.tolist() print("Top features by XGBoost embedded importance:") print(final_features) # Final feature set X_final = Xr_s[final_features].copy() X_final.corr().iplot( kind="heatmap", title="Final Selected Features Correlations" )

Part C¶

Model Building, Tuning and Evaluation
Predicting Positive Market Moves Using Support Vector Support Vector Machine (SVM)

C1.) Data setup for final modelling¶

In [22]:
# Loading patched dataset
if PARQ.exists():
    df = pd.read_parquet(PARQ)
else:
    df = pd.read_csv(CSV, parse_dates=["Date"]).set_index("Date")

df = df[df.index >= SAMPLE_START].copy()

print(df.shape)

# use previously selected final features 
final_features = final_features 

TARGET = 'y'
X_final = df[final_features].copy()
y_final = df[TARGET].astype(int)

print("Final feature shape:", X_final.shape)
print("Positive class proportion:", y_final.mean().round(3))

(2804, 50)
Final feature shape: (2804, 11)
Positive class proportion: 0.326

C2.) Chronological train/test split¶

In [23]:
TEST_PCT = 0.2  # 20% test
split_idx = int(len(X_final) * (1 - TEST_PCT))

X_train, X_test = X_final.iloc[:split_idx], X_final.iloc[split_idx:]
y_train, y_test = y_final.iloc[:split_idx], y_final.iloc[split_idx:]

print("Train:", X_train.shape, "Test:", X_test.shape)
print("Train range:", X_train.index.min(), "→", X_train.index.max())
print("Test  range:", X_test.index.min(),  "→", X_test.index.max())

Train: (2243, 11) Test: (561, 11)
Train range: 2015-01-01 00:00:00 → 2023-08-07 00:00:00
Test  range: 2023-08-08 00:00:00 → 2025-09-30 00:00:00

C3.) SVM pipeline (imputer + scaler + SVC)¶

In [24]:
#### C3.) SVM pipeline (imputer + scaler + SVC)

svm_pipe = Pipeline(steps=[
    ("imputer", SimpleImputer(strategy="median")),
    ("scaler", StandardScaler()),
    ("clf", SVC(probability=True, class_weight="balanced", random_state=42))
])

svm_pipe.fit(X_train, y_train)
Out[24]:
Pipeline(steps=[('imputer', SimpleImputer(strategy='median')),
                ('scaler', StandardScaler()),
                ('clf',
                 SVC(class_weight='balanced', probability=True,
                     random_state=42))])
In a Jupyter environment, please rerun this cell to show the HTML representation or trust the notebook.
On GitHub, the HTML representation is unable to render, please try loading this page with nbviewer.org.
Parameters
steps  [('imputer', ...), ('scaler', ...), ...]
transform_input  None
memory  None
verbose  False
Parameters
missing_values  nan
strategy  'median'
fill_value  None
copy  True
add_indicator  False
keep_empty_features  False
Parameters
copy  True
with_mean  True
with_std  True
Parameters
C  1.0
kernel  'rbf'
degree  3
gamma  'scale'
coef0  0.0
shrinking  True
probability  True
tol  0.001
cache_size  200
class_weight  'balanced'
verbose  False
max_iter  -1
decision_function_shape  'ovr'
break_ties  False
random_state  42

C4.) Hyperparameter tuning with Time-Series CV and Grid Search method¶

In [25]:
#### C4.) Hyperparameter tuning with Time-Series CV and Grid Search method
tscv = TimeSeriesSplit(n_splits=5)
param_grid = {
    "clf__kernel": ["rbf", "poly"],
    "clf__C": [0.5, 1, 2, 5, 10],
    "clf__gamma": ["scale", 0.1, 0.05, 0.01],
    "clf__degree": [2, 3]  # only used if kernel='poly'
}

scoring = {"roc_auc": "roc_auc", "f1": "f1", "accuracy": "accuracy"}

grid = GridSearchCV(
    estimator=svm_pipe,
    param_grid=param_grid,
    scoring=scoring,
    refit="roc_auc",     # optimize for ROC-AUC
    cv=tscv,
    verbose=2,
    n_jobs=-1
)

grid.fit(X_train, y_train)

print("Best params:", grid.best_params_)
print("Best cross-val ROC-AUC:", round(grid.best_score_, 4))

Fitting 5 folds for each of 80 candidates, totalling 400 fits
Best params: {'clf__C': 0.5, 'clf__degree': 2, 'clf__gamma': 0.01, 'clf__kernel': 'rbf'}
Best cross-val ROC-AUC: 0.605

C5.) Evaluate on held-out test set¶

In [26]:
# --- Test set evaluation ---

best_model = grid.best_estimator_
y_pred = best_model.predict(X_test)
y_proba = best_model.predict_proba(X_test)[:, 1]

# --- Metrics ---
print("Test ROC-AUC:", round(roc_auc_score(y_test, y_proba), 4))
print("Accuracy:", round(accuracy_score(y_test, y_pred), 4))
print("Precision:", round(precision_score(y_test, y_pred), 4))
print("Recall:", round(recall_score(y_test, y_pred), 4))
print("F1:", round(f1_score(y_test, y_pred), 4))

print("\nClassification Report:\n", classification_report(y_test, y_pred, digits=3))

Test ROC-AUC: 0.6129
Accuracy: 0.5419
Precision: 0.3861
Recall: 0.6595
F1: 0.487

Classification Report:
               precision    recall  f1-score   support

           0      0.743     0.484     0.586       376
           1      0.386     0.659     0.487       185

    accuracy                          0.542       561
   macro avg      0.564     0.572     0.537       561
weighted avg      0.625     0.542     0.553       561

C6.) ROC Curve¶

In [27]:
fpr, tpr, _ = roc_curve(y_test, y_proba)
plt.figure(figsize=(5,4))
plt.plot(fpr, tpr, label=f"ROC curve (AUC = {roc_auc_score(y_test, y_proba):.3f})")
plt.plot([0,1],[0,1],"--",color="gray")
plt.xlabel("False Positive Rate")
plt.ylabel("True Positive Rate")
plt.title("SVM ROC Curve")
plt.legend()
plt.show()

C7.) Confusion Matrix¶

In [28]:
# Display confussion matrix
disp = ConfusionMatrixDisplay.from_estimator(
        best_model,
        X_test,
        y_test,
        # display_labels=model.classes_,
        cmap=plt.cm.Blues
    )
plt.title('Confusion matrix')
plt.show()

Trading Strategy¶

We will now define a trading strategy based on the predicted signals from our model. These signals will determine whether to enter a buy or sell position. The performance of this strategy will then be compared against a buy-and-hold strategy to evaluate its effectiveness. Finally, we will visualize and analyze the results to assess how well the machine learning algorithm performs in a real-world trading scenario.

In [29]:
# Subsume into a new dataframe
df2 = X_final.copy()                              # df[-len(X_test)
df2['Signal'] = best_model.predict(X_final)        # tunedmodel.predict(X_test)
In [30]:
X_final.columns
Out[30]:
Index(['vix_level', 'snp500_ret_1', 'qqq_rsi_20', 'risk_aversion_proxy',
       'tlt_ret_5', 'qqq_vol_20', 'qqq_vol_z', 'qqq_logret_1', 'qqq_lb_5',
       'qqq_volume', 'qqq_atr_20'],
      dtype='object')
In [31]:
# Daily Returns - Benchmark return
df2['Returns'] = dailyReturn(df[f'{MAIN_TICKER}_close']).shift(-1)

# Strategy Returns - Logistic
df2['Strategy'] =  df2['Returns'] * df2['Signal'].shift(1).fillna(0)

# Localize index for pyfolio
df2.index = df2.index.tz_localize('utc')
# Check the output
# df2.shape
# df2.head(5)
In [32]:
# Create Tear sheet using pyfolio for outsample - for X_test
pf.create_simple_tear_sheet(df2['Strategy'])
